elements.subsection-2411-021: Information Theoretic versus Computation Constrained Models
elements.sent-2411-717: The study of property testing can be broadly divided into two main categories: information theoretic and computation constrained testing.
elements.sent-2411-718: In the former category, no computation assumption is made about the tester, one can think the tester is computationally unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-719: In the latter category, we impose that a tester has to be generated uniformly by a Turing machine, and it has to obey the computation resource constraints of the corresponding complexity class (\eg in this model a tester for $\propQMA$ is required to be a $\BQP$ verifier).
elements.sent-2411-720: In particular, these models can capture the following behavior regarding property testing and computation complexity.
elements.sent-2411-721: Information theoretic: captures the inherent limitations imposed by quantum/classical information theory regardless of any computation limitations on a tester.
elements.sent-2411-722: Any language or promise problem in a complexity (or computability) class with classical inputs gives rise to two disjoint collection of bit strings $L_{yes}$ and $L_{no}$ consisting in yes and no instances, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-723: We remark that the information theoretic version of the class $\propBPP$ and $\propBQP$ trivially capture them.
elements.sent-2411-724: By considering classical input states in the complexity constrained models of property testing, we can ask whether a classical bit string (given as input state to be tested) is a yes or no instance of a language or promise problem.
elements.sent-2411-725: Therefore, these models capture standard complexity classes.
elements.sent-2411-726: Under the assumption $\BQP \ne \QMA$, we have $\propBQP \ne \propQMA$ for their computation constrained models.
elements.sent-2411-727: In contrast, for the information theoretic models, we have the collapse $\propBQP = \propQMA$.
